summary.objective: To investigate how geometric disorder from randomly distributed static obstacles on a 2D square lattice controls collective chase-escape dynamics, linking spatial connectivity to pursuit efficiency.
summary.methods: Used a combination of geometric Breadth-First Search analysis, dynamical observables (trapping time and capture cost), survival statistics via Weibull distribution fitting, and transport characterization through mean-squared displacement exponent on an agent-based lattice model with short-range sensing rules.
summary.results: Observed non-monotonic dependence of trapping time and cost on obstacle density, with a maximum near percolation due to path elongation versus fragmentation; survival decay followed Weibull statistics, showing crossovers from compressed to stretched exponential regimes; transport became subdiffusive and converged near percolation, all indicating lattice connectivity as a key control parameter.
summary.conclusion: Chase-escape dynamics in disordered environments are governed by a geometry-driven crossover, where percolation and connectivity unify the spatial, temporal, and collective behavior, highlighting the role of geometric constraints in active matter systems.
